pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-23

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Do not expand coverage via broad runs until failures are clustered; smaller reviewed batches made repeated citation issues easier to group and yielded more actionable prompt changes.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 explicitly says teams need smaller eval batches with visible failure clusters before expanding coverage, and that smaller reviewed batches made repeated citation issues easier to group and produced more actionable prompt changes.

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewer throughput and perceived speed regressed in the last eval review because failures were not grouped by pattern; reviewers preferred fewer cases with clearer evidence over larger runs requiring manual interpretation per row.
claimJudgments.explanation: S5 states the last eval review felt slower than expected because failures were not grouped by pattern, and reviewers preferred fewer cases with clearer evidence over a larger run where every row required manual interpretation.

claimJudgments.claimText: Coverage improvements can mask regressions in grounding; citation support needs an explicit shipping gate that blocks releases when outputs contain unsupported claims.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 says coverage can improve while grounding regresses and recommends an explicit shipping gate/release block when candidate outputs make unsupported claims.

claimJudgments.claimText: Long traces are valuable for debugging why a claim failed, but uncontrolled retries drove a 1.34x cost increase; trace retention is acceptable if retries are capped and summarized in the run manifest (retry count, cost ratio, and what the trace resolved).
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 provides all elements: long traces help debug failures; unchecked retries created a 1.34x cost increase; finance accepted trace retention only if retries are capped and summarized in the run manifest including retry count, cost ratio, and what the trace helped resolve.

claimJudgments.claimText: Holdout cases should be visible only as summaries/aggregate scores in dashboards; labels/expected answers must stay out of prompt-iteration workflows to reduce memorization risk.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 states holdouts may appear in dashboards as summaries/aggregate scores, but tuning labels/expected answers should stay out of prompt-iteration workflows to avoid memorization/overfitting to visible examples.

missingImportantEvidence: S6 explicitly recommends 'one more visible batch before live orchestration' and frames expansion as acceptable only if grounding improves without exceeding cost guardrail; the recommendation matches this but does not cite S6.

S5 asks for a dashboard clustering taxonomy separating unsupported claims, weak citations, cost regressions, and holdout concerns; the recommendation references clustering taxonomy as an open question but does not cite S5/S1 in the recommendation text itself.
recommendationJudgment.explanation: The generated recommendation is consistent with the packet: stage expansion with a smaller visible batch (supported by S1 and also S6), enforce a grounding/citation shipping gate (S2), cap retries and summarize in a manifest to control costs while keeping traces (S3), and preserve holdout integrity via aggregate-only reporting and hiding labels from tuning (S4). Claims are well-supported; main issue is minor: a key source (S6) that directly supports the 'one more visible batch before live orchestration' framing is not cited, though the idea is still supported indirectly by S1 and aligns with the packet overall.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
